Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study later, and then later never really came until things got urgent. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when I remembered something was due. I spent maybe 17 hours a week on schoolwork, but it was scattered and unfocused. I just kind of drifted through without a real plan.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly cram when a deadline was breathing down my neck. Regular study times weren't really my thing—I'd get distracted by other stuff, or just not feel like it. Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me into action.
Q3: Usually it was because I got an email notification about something being due soon, or I vaguely remembered there was an assignment I hadn't started. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything major. It wasn't like I had a habit of going there—more like panic-driven visits when I realized I was falling behind.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried a few times to make it a habit, like checking in every morning, but it never stuck. The LMS just felt like another thing I had to deal with, not something that fit naturally into my day. I'd forget about it for a while, then feel guilty when I finally logged back in and saw all the stuff I'd missed.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful for me since I learn better from text than videos. When I actually sat down and read through the course notes, things made more sense. The assignment descriptions were helpful too, at least when I bothered to read them carefully. I liked being able to go at my own pace with written stuff.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—posting there felt pointless to me, like I was just talking into a void. The videos I'd often skip or let play in the background without really watching. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment grade, I probably ignored it. It just felt like extra work that wouldn't pay off.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection between the activity and my grade, then it felt worth doing. Anything that seemed like busywork or optional enrichment got pushed to the bottom of my list. I needed to see immediate relevance, otherwise my brain just checked out. Time felt too limited to waste on things that wouldn't directly help me pass.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. If it was required, I'd eventually do it—maybe late, but I'd get there. Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, especially when I was already struggling to keep up with the mandatory stuff. There was no real motivation to go above and beyond.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my laptop so I could look at them later without being online. Sometimes I'd study from notes I'd written down or use Google to understand concepts the course materials didn't explain well. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me and my notes or random websites.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour or two going over printed notes or re-reading stuff I'd downloaded, but none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also think through problems in my head while doing other things, like walking or lying in bed. The LMS only captures clicks, not actual mental effort or understanding.
Q11: Yeah, that happened a lot. I'd download a reading once, then work through it over several days offline. Or I'd screenshot important slides and review them on my phone later. The LMS would just show one brief access, but I might have spent hours with that material afterward. It's not a great measure of actual study time.
Q12: Around midterms, everything kind of fell apart for me. I had stuff going on personally, and the course material was getting harder. I stopped attending class almost entirely—my attendance was terrible, like under 10%. I just felt disconnected and couldn't find the energy to catch up once I'd fallen behind.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed about other things, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Competing responsibilities made it easy to justify skipping course stuff, like telling myself I'd do it tomorrow. Confusion about the material also made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how lost I was.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video or reading and just stare at it without really absorbing anything. My mind would wander, or I'd be too tired to focus. Technically I was 'engaging' with the material, but nothing was actually sticking. Those sessions felt like going through the motions.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but keep putting it off because it felt overwhelming or boring. Sometimes I'd avoid things because I didn't understand the instructions and felt too embarrassed to ask for help. Procrastination was basically my default mode, even when I knew it would hurt me later.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really got me moving. I'd ignore stuff until the last possible moment, then scramble to get something submitted. The LMS became a lot more active for me in the 24 hours before something was due. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done anything at all.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me, honestly. By the time I got comments back, I'd already moved on mentally. Sometimes good feedback made me feel briefly motivated, but it faded fast. Bad feedback just made me want to avoid the course even more. It didn't create any lasting change in my habits.
Q18: The course structure felt kind of loose, which didn't help someone like me who needs external pressure to stay on track. There weren't enough checkpoints or reminders to keep me engaged throughout. I think I would have done better with more frequent small deadlines instead of a few big ones. The flexibility actually worked against me.
Q19: The lack of clear structure made engagement harder for me. When everything was available at once, I didn't know where to start and ended up starting nowhere. More guided pathways or weekly checklists would have helped. On the positive side, having readings available as downloads was convenient for my learning style.
Q20: I think some students might be like me—clicking around, opening things, but not really processing anything. High activity could just mean someone's anxious and checking things repeatedly, or they're lost and searching for answers. It doesn't necessarily mean productive engagement. You can be very active and still not learn much.
Q21: That makes sense to me. Someone could download everything at the start, study offline, and barely touch the LMS again while still doing okay. Or they might be naturally quick learners who don't need to spend hours on the platform. Low activity doesn't always mean low effort—it might just mean efficient or offline studying.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked, but they miss everything that happens in your head or outside the platform. They can't measure understanding, confusion, or genuine interest. They also miss offline studying, conversations with classmates, or just thinking through problems. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real mental effort. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being curious and trying to understand. I wasn't really engaged this semester, even when I was technically doing the work. My heart wasn't in it.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how disconnected and unmotivated I felt most of the time. It wouldn't capture the guilt I felt about falling behind, or the stress that made me avoid the course. My actual emotional state and the reasons behind my disengagement are invisible in the logs. Numbers don't tell the whole story of why someone drifts away.
